does: This paper adapts Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) to measure how well Large Language Models and Vision Language Models align with human cognitive representations. The method collects pairwise similarity ratings from both AI systems and humans across semantic tasks involving text and images, then quantifies alignment by comparing these rating patterns. Testing on multiple models including GPT-4o, the authors find that GPT-4o demonstrates the strongest overall alignment with human group-level judgments, particularly when processing text rather than images. Importantly, they show that no tested model adequately captures the individual differences in how humans perceive semantic similarity, indicating a significant gap between AI and human representational spaces at the individual level.
connects: This work builds on decades of cognitive neuroscience research using RSA to compare neural representations across brains and with computational models, extending these methods to evaluate AI alignment with human cognition. It responds to growing concerns about deploying LLMs in high-stakes decision-making roles and the need for behavioral measurement tools that go beyond accuracy metrics. The paper connects to recent work on evaluating model capabilities and safety, which has typically relied on benchmarks testing task performance rather than representational alignment. It also engages with literature on cross-modal understanding in multimodal AI systems and the challenge of capturing human behavioral variability in alignment assessments.
enables: This flexible RSA framework enables future work to systematically map representational gaps between different AI systems and human cognition across diverse domains and tasks. The finding that models fail to capture individual human differences suggests important directions for personalized AI alignment methods that account for cognitive diversity. The approach could facilitate evaluation of newer models and serve as a behavioral diagnostic tool for identifying which representational aspects of AI systems diverge most significantly from human understanding. This work also enables investigation of whether models trained with human feedback actually align with human representations or merely human-preferred outputs, and could inform design of training procedures that encourage more human-like internal representations rather than just human-aligned behavior.
